title: Underlying rules of evolutionary urban systems in Africa

data_scope.spatial_coverage: continental Africa (54 countries) under one unified urban-agglomeration definition
data_scope.units: African urban agglomerations (exact count not stated in available text; set defined by Africapolis methodology)
data_scope.key_variables: city population and built-up area per agglomeration; continental urban population/urbanization level; country-level GDP per capita

core_conclusion: Across 1950-2020 Africa's urban systems follow three simple rules under one unified continental city definition: Zipf's law shows growing population concentration in large cities (dispersed -> centralized distributions); Gibrat's law holds since the 1990s (growth independent of initial size); and the urban scaling law reveals unexpected super-linear scaling of built-up area with population (lower land-use efficiency in large cities, especially Eastern and Western Africa), with declining scaling exponents over time signaling maturing economies of scale.
why_it_matters: Africa will drive global urbanization yet its urban-system evolution is understudied; the paper supplies a 70-year continental record, jointly applies three classical laws on a consistent dataset, surfaces a counter-intuitive super-linear land-scaling result, and gives planners an evidence base for compact-growth and land-management policy.
why_nature: Reduces continental urban complexity to three transferable statistical laws; addresses a globally consequential, under-served region; uses a unified definition that enables fair cross-country comparison; conclusions are strongly policy-translatable.
added_value_to_my_research: Explain built-up scaling residuals via SHAP/MGWR -> diagnostic of why cities sprawl
Calibrate a generative Gibrat growth equation -> Monte-Carlo/agent-based urban simulator
Couple city-size imbalance with 2SFCA/R2SFCA -> service-access and health equity by city tier
Fuse Africapolis with RS built-up volume -> reproducible multi-source fusion, area vs volume
Learn the urban hierarchy as a physics-informed inter-city graph -> agglomeration network detection
